ai_strategy_lead.name: Mark D. West
ai_strategy_lead.title: Provost and Executive Vice Chancellor for Academic Affairs (leads +AI initiative); supported by Betsy Sinclair, Nathan Jacobs, Jen Smith, and Peter Boumgarden

documents.title: DI2 Accelerator AI Coordinating Committee charter
McKelvey Engineering AI Signature Initiative (strategic plan)

ai_strategy.summary: WashU's AI strategy is anchored by the universitywide +AI academic initiative launched March 4, 2026, led by Provost Mark D. West. The strategy pursues four goals: fuel student success, transform teaching and learning, accelerate world-class research, and support the St. Louis region, while emphasizing human judgment and responsible use. Execution is coordinated by the Digital Intelligence & Innovation (DI2) Accelerator and a newly chartered AI Coordinating Committee (launched Fall 2025) reporting to the Chancellor, which is tasked with a 12-month AI roadmap. The Center for Health AI (2024) and the Center for Computational and AI-enabled Imaging Sciences (2025) serve as flagship research structures, while McKelvey Engineering's signature AI initiative ties AI research into domain-specific problem solving.

key_provisions: Do not enter WashU intellectual property or secure data (including deidentified healthcare data) into non-approved AI platforms
Approved secure tools (Secure WashU ChatGPT, Ask WashU Beta, Gemini for Education, ChatGPT Edu, Secure AI API Endpoints, Box AI, Zoom AI Companion) are HIPAA/FERPA protected
All new AI tools must go through an Office of Information Security Vendor Security Review before institutional use/purchase
Faculty set course-level syllabus AI policies using CTL templates (four stances: allowed without restriction, allowed with citation, partially restricted, fully restricted)
Academic integrity expectations require students to review course expectations for authorized AI use and seek instructor clarification
Institutional ethics review committee (via CISO) uses NIST AI Risk Management Framework baseline controls to vet AI use cases
ai_policy.summary: WashU operates a comprehensive AI policy framework combining institution-level guardrails (vendor review, data classification, NIST AI RMF-aligned risk assessment) with distributed instructor discretion at the course level. The Center for Teaching and Learning publishes four standard syllabus stances faculty can adopt. WashU IT maintains an approved tools catalog with clearly labeled HIPAA/FERPA-protected options and explicit prohibitions on entering sensitive data into non-approved platforms.

bodies.name: AI Coordinating Committee
bodies.scope: Cohesive, responsible, strategic AI adoption across the university; identifies high-impact opportunities, establishes ethics guidelines, advises on data governance, recommends resource allocation; reports directly to the Chancellor. Members include Office of the Provost, IT, Digital Transformation, school deans, WashU-BJC Center for Health AI, Epic/HIP leadership, and at-large faculty. Chartered for a 12-month roadmap.

bodies.name: +AI Initiative Leadership Team (Office of the Provost)
bodies.scope: Drives the universitywide +AI academic initiative. Led by Provost Mark D. West with AVPs Betsy Sinclair and Nathan Jacobs (Digital Transformation), VP Jen Smith (Educational Initiatives), and AVP Peter Boumgarden.

bodies.name: Digital Intelligence & Innovation (DI2) Accelerator
bodies.scope: Operational hub for AI initiatives including AI tool rollout, Digital Transformation Research Corps, AI Curriculum Corps, and AI education programming. Executive director Dan Maranan.

bodies.name: AI Ethics Review Committee
bodies.scope: Supplements existing IT governance bodies across administration, teaching/learning, research, and clinical operations. Uses structured rubrics and NIST AI RMF 72 requirements as baseline. Led operationally by WashU's CISO who drafted the institution-wide AI risk management policy.

bodies.name: Academic AI Working Group (Office of the Provost)
bodies.scope: Faculty insight groups exploring AI opportunities and considerations; informs policy and pedagogical practice around generative AI.

governance_structure.summary: WashU employs a multi-tier AI governance model. The AI Coordinating Committee (launched Fall 2025) reports directly to the Chancellor and serves as the strategic coordinating body. The Provost-led +AI Initiative Leadership Team drives academic strategy, while the DI2 Accelerator is the operational engine for tool rollout and programs. A separate AI Ethics Review Committee, led by the CISO and aligned with NIST AI RMF, handles risk assessment. Notably, Philip Payne serves as a dedicated Chief Health AI Officer for WashU Medicine and BJC Health through their joint Center for Health AI.

initiatives.title: +AI Universitywide Academic Initiative Launch
initiatives.description: On March 4, 2026, WashU announced a universitywide academic initiative called +AI, led by Provost Mark D. West. The initiative is framed around four pillars: fuel student success, transform teaching and learning, accelerate world-class research, and support the St. Louis region. It includes the AI Literacy for the WashU Scholar Canvas module, the AI Curriculum Corps, institutionally supported AI tools, faculty insight groups, and +AI Perspectives Week (March 2026) featuring The Atlantic CEO Nicholas Thompson.
initiatives.significance: Establishes WashU's first fully integrated, universitywide AI strategy explicitly framed to balance AI enthusiasm with human judgment and institutional values.

investment.funding_source: Office of the Provost; DI2 Accelerator
investment.description: Universitywide program with dedicated leadership team spanning multiple vice provost offices; specific dollar amount not disclosed publicly.

collaborations.description: Cross-functional partnership across provost office, IT, teaching and learning, and libraries.

tools_and_vendors.name: ChatGPT Edu
tools_and_vendors.role: Advanced AI tool for undergrads who complete AI Literacy module

tools_and_vendors.name: Gemini for Education
tools_and_vendors.role: Free secure AI tool for Danforth Campus

initiatives.policy_implications: Sets the strategic frame for all downstream AI policy and practice across the university.

initiatives.title: AI Literacy for the WashU Scholar Canvas Module
initiatives.description: A 30-minute self-enrollment Canvas module for undergraduate and graduate students on the Danforth Campus providing a shared foundation for understanding, critically evaluating, and responsibly using generative AI. Students who complete the module receive free access to ChatGPT Edu. Over 1,000 students had completed the module as of March 2026.
initiatives.significance: Creates an institutional AI literacy baseline tied directly to tool access, effectively making AI literacy a de facto prerequisite for enhanced generative AI privileges.

investment.description: Canvas-based self-paced module with tied tool entitlement; development cost not disclosed.

collaborations.description: ChatGPT Edu access tied to module completion.

initiatives.policy_implications: Establishes a literacy-gated access model, distinct from universal rollout or full restriction.

initiatives.title: Secure WashU ChatGPT Rollout
initiatives.description: In December 2023, WashU launched its own secure, HIPAA- and FERPA-protected version of ChatGPT (currently ChatGPT-4o) free to all students, faculty, and staff. Developed jointly by the DI2 Accelerator and WashU IT, the tool prohibits OpenAI/Microsoft from viewing or training on user data. An upgrade to GPT-4o was deployed in October 2024. A complementary Secure Ask WashU Beta adds multi-file support and model selection.
initiatives.significance: Early R1-scale deployment of a closed, regulated-data-eligible LLM interface tied to institutional identity and vendor terms.

investment.description: Custom secure LLM deployment via OpenAI/Microsoft partnership with contractual data-use restrictions.

collaborations.description: Negotiated agreement preventing vendor viewing or training on WashU data.

tools_and_vendors.name: Secure Ask WashU Beta
tools_and_vendors.role: Advanced chatbot with multi-file, multi-model support

initiatives.policy_implications: Demonstrates the preferred institutional model: approved closed tools for sensitive data, with public tools allowed only for non-sensitive inputs.

initiatives.title: Center for Health AI (CHAI) Launch with BJC Health System
initiatives.description: On November 13, 2024, WashU Medicine and BJC Health System jointly launched the Center for Health AI, led by Philip R.O. Payne as founding director and Chief Health AI Officer. Clinical focus areas include workflow and documentation automation, sepsis/chemo risk prediction, medical imaging interpretation, blood loss prediction in surgery, and scheduling/resource optimization. Operating team includes Paul Scheel, Chris Miller, CIO Jessie Minton, and Jerry Fox.
initiatives.significance: One of the earliest major academic medical center partnerships with a large community health system establishing joint AI governance and a dedicated Chief Health AI Officer.

investment.funding_source: WashU Medicine and BJC Health System (joint)
investment.description: Joint investment; specific dollar amount not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Joint academic-health system AI center with shared governance.

tools_and_vendors.name: Epic
tools_and_vendors.role: Integrated into governance via Epic/HIP leadership on AI Coordinating Committee

initiatives.policy_implications: Establishes a model for AMC–health system joint AI governance at scale.

initiatives.title: Center for Computational and AI-enabled Imaging Sciences
initiatives.description: Announced November 2025 by the Mallinckrodt Institute of Radiology at WashU Medicine, the new center is directed by Mark Anastasio (Mallinckrodt Endowed Professor of Imaging Sciences). It develops AI-based imaging tools for diagnosis and precision treatment of cancers, cardiovascular disease, and neurological disease, with partners across McKelvey Engineering, Siteman Cancer Center, and Medicine/Neurology/Psychiatry/Radiation Oncology.
initiatives.significance: Vertically integrated imaging AI center bridging clinical radiology with core computer-vision and ML research.

investment.funding_source: Mallinckrodt Institute of Radiology; McKelvey School of Engineering
investment.description: New center standing up with endowed professorial leadership; cross-school support.

initiatives.title: AI for Health Institute and AI for Health Seed Grant
initiatives.description: The AI for Health Institute connects AI researchers with health investigators across WashU to advance AI in health. Its AI for Health Seed Grant program awards up to $50,000 per project for interdisciplinary AI+health research; FY2026 proposals were due March 4, 2026. Research supported includes surgical AI, spinal cord disease detection, cognitive assistance, and healthcare communication.
initiatives.significance: Institutional seed funding mechanism translating cross-disciplinary AI+health collaboration into funded pilot projects.

investment.funding_source: WashU internal (Here and Next Seed Grant program; AI for Health Institute)
investment.description: Per-project seed grants of up to $50,000; total annual program budget not disclosed.

partners: Institute for Informatics, Data Science and Biostatistics (I2DB)

initiatives.title: Digital Transformation Research Corps
initiatives.description: A 10-week summer DI2 Accelerator program that pairs WashU faculty-led research teams with student developers and data scientists to create digital tools and AI models. The 2026 cohort will accelerate 18 faculty-led research projects, more than doubling the scale of the 2025 pilot. Applications for 2026 were due January 15, 2026.
initiatives.significance: Cross-disciplinary research-accelerator model translating AI expertise into domain research artifacts.

investment.description: Student support, technical mentorship, and computing resources; budget not disclosed.

initiatives.title: AI Curriculum Corps (Summer 2026)
initiatives.description: Launching Summer 2026, the AI Curriculum Corps is a DI2-supported faculty grant program that helps faculty adapt and create courses using generative AI to advance pedagogical goals across disciplines. Supported by the provost's +AI initiative and multiple partner units.
initiatives.significance: Direct institutional investment in retooling curriculum around generative AI rather than only in retrofitting policy.

investment.funding_source: DI2 Accelerator; Office of the Provost

initiatives.title: WashU Law Global AI Partnership
initiatives.description: Beginning 2026, WashU School of Law launched a global network connecting WashU Law with nine preeminent legal programs across six continents (University of Queensland, Fudan University, Universidad de la Sabana, Addis Ababa University, National Law School of India University, Utrecht University, Comillas University, Koç University, University of Nottingham). Components include virtual webinars on AI regulation, joint research forums, and in-person faculty/student training. Dean Stephanie Lindquist leads.
initiatives.significance: Positions WashU Law as a global hub for AI-and-law education — one of the few international AI education alliances among U.S. law schools.

investment.description: Multi-institution international network; amounts not disclosed.

partners: Addis Ababa University School of Law
National Law School of India University
collaborations.description: Nine-institution global AI-and-law alliance across six continents.

initiatives.policy_implications: Builds global AI regulatory literacy among future lawyers, judges, and policymakers.

initiatives.title: WashU + Mayfield AI Garage Partnership
initiatives.description: Announced February 24, 2026, Silicon Valley VC Mayfield partnered with WashU's Skandalaris Center and McKelvey School of Engineering to run an AI Garage program (May-November 2026). Winning student teams are hosted at the Skandalaris Center with mentorship, workspace, academic credits, and VC access. WashU is only the second university (after Berkeley) to join Mayfield's AI Garage.
initiatives.significance: Direct institutional-VC pipeline for student AI startup formation; rare second-mover into Mayfield's flagship university AI program.

investment.description: VC-backed incubation; specific dollar amount not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Venture capital partnership with Silicon Valley AI-focused VC.

initiatives.title: Fundamentals of AI Professional Certificate (CAPS + DI2)
initiatives.description: Launched September 29, 2025 as a 9-week non-credit professional certificate by WashU Continuing & Professional Studies (CAPS) with the DI2 Accelerator. Designed for professionals and lifelong learners with no coding experience; three hands-on modules cover AI tools, core AI concepts, and ethics/bias/accountability. Led by DI2 executive director Dan Maranan.
initiatives.significance: Extends AI upskilling beyond enrolled students to working professionals in the St. Louis region.

initiatives.title: NSF AI-ACCESS Training Grant ($3M, 5 years)
initiatives.description: The National Science Foundation is investing $3 million over five years in WashU's AI-ACCESS program (Artificial Intelligence Advancements and Convergence in Computational, Environmental and Social Sciences). WashU will recruit 49 doctoral students (24 NSF-funded, 25 WashU-funded) trained at the intersection of AI, environmental science, and social science to address climate change and similar global challenges.
initiatives.significance: Externally validated convergent AI training grant channeling PhD pipeline into climate/environmental AI applications.

investment.description: NSF Research Traineeship award over 5 years.

initiatives.title: AI Ethics Review Committee and NIST AI RMF Risk Governance
initiatives.description: WashU's CISO led drafting a universitywide AI risk management policy and stood up an AI ethics review committee. With WWT consulting support, WashU evaluated ~20 active AI use cases (8 deep-dived) using a multi-lens rubric (auditor, attacker, risk officer, innovator) and the NIST AI RMF's 72 baseline requirements. The work reduced shadow AI and tool sprawl and established a structured intake/review process.
initiatives.significance: Operational, NIST-aligned AI risk governance inside an R1 private university — one of the earlier documented adoptions of NIST AI RMF at scale in higher ed.

investment.funding_source: WashU IT; Office of Information Security
investment.description: Internal risk/compliance investment with external consulting support from WWT.

initiatives.policy_implications: Standardizes how WashU evaluates every new AI use case and procurement.

initiatives.title: Center for Trustworthy AI in Cyberphysical Systems
initiatives.description: A McKelvey Engineering research center developing methods, tools, and integrated systems that make AI-driven cyberphysical systems (autonomous vehicles, drones, delivery systems) secure, safe, reliable, and resilient. Includes four core research areas (secure CPS, hardware security, systems, resilience). Faculty include Ning Zhang.
initiatives.significance: Institutional research capability for AI-CPS security — a growing national concern as AI enters physical systems.

investment.funding_source: McKelvey School of Engineering; federal grants
investment.description: Standing research center; specific budget not disclosed.

initiatives.title: Center for High Performance Computing (CHPC) with 100+ NVIDIA GPUs
initiatives.description: Operated by the Research Computing and Informatics Facility (RCIF), CHPC provides massive-scale compute to WashU's imaging and AI research community with 100+ datacenter-class NVIDIA GPUs (SXM H100, A100 80G/40G; PCIe A100, V100S, V100, T4, K20). Infrastructure includes 3 petaflops of GPU-accelerated capacity, HDR InfiniBand interconnect, 18+ PB of tiered storage, and SLURM scheduling.
initiatives.significance: One of the larger university-operated GPU-accelerated HPC clusters among private R1s, enabling large-model and imaging AI at scale.

investment.funding_source: WashU RCIF; federal grants; departmental buy-in
investment.description: Multi-PB storage and 3 PF GPU capacity; exact capital investment not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Hardware vendor relationships for GPU/CPU infrastructure.

Scheduled via SLURM. Supports imaging, ML, and large-model research. McKelvey Engineering also operates a separate EIT compute cluster.

regulatory_context.summary: Missouri has no statewide higher education AI policy as of early 2026. The Missouri Department of Elementary and Secondary Education has issued K-12 guidance, but higher education institutions set their own policies. As a private institution, WashU does not sit within a state university system; its policy framework is entirely institutional. Six of Missouri's eleven public four-year universities have AI policies/guidelines; WashU has adopted a comprehensive private-institution framework that exceeds the state baseline.

narrative_summary: Washington University in St. Louis is building a mature, multi-layered AI posture anchored in the March 2026 launch of the universitywide +AI Academic Initiative, led by Provost Mark D. West. The initiative is intentionally positioned to balance enthusiasm and caution: Chancellor Andrew D. Martin frames the challenge as one of 'appropriate fit' for AI in the research and educational mission, and West emphasizes human judgment over 'alarm nor unexamined enthusiasm.' Governance is multi-tier — a new AI Coordinating Committee chartered Fall 2025 reports directly to the Chancellor and is tasked with a 12-month roadmap; the Provost's +AI leadership team includes AVPs Sinclair, Jacobs, Smith, and Boumgarden; the DI2 Accelerator is the operational hub; and a CISO-led AI ethics review committee, aligned with the NIST AI RMF's 72 controls (developed with WWT), governs risk. WashU uniquely holds a dedicated Chief Health AI Officer role — Philip R.O. Payne — leading the joint WashU Medicine/BJC Health Center for Health AI (2024), with a companion Center for Computational and AI-enabled Imaging Sciences launched November 2025 at the Mallinckrodt Institute of Radiology. Enterprise tooling is comprehensive: Secure WashU ChatGPT (2023, GPT-4o), ChatGPT Edu, Gemini for Education, NotebookLM, Microsoft Copilot, Box AI, and Ask WashU Beta are all institutionally supported with clear HIPAA/FERPA labels and a vendor-review process for anything new. Research capacity rests on the CHPC HPC cluster (100+ datacenter GPUs, ~3 PF, 18+ PB tiered storage), McKelvey's AI signature initiative, the Center for Trustworthy AI in CPS, and the AI for Health Institute's $50K seed grants. Workforce programs span the CAPS Fundamentals of AI certificate, the AI Curriculum Corps, the Digital Transformation Research Corps (18 projects in 2026), and the Mayfield AI Garage partnership (second university after Berkeley). WashU Law launched a nine-institution global AI and Law network in 2026. Policy stance is comprehensive: course-level syllabus templates from the CTL (four stances), institutional acceptable use rules, and an AI Literacy module that ties ChatGPT Edu access to completion — a distinctive literacy-gated access model. The main gaps are public disclosure of dollar figures for the +AI initiative and any student-retention predictive analytics work, which do not appear to be publicly documented.
